english: ṕyir (phyir) even for the most trifling matter Stg.; ṕyi-rol-gyi (phyi rol gyi) zaṅ-ziṅ (zang zing) external goods, earthly possessions, (opp. to internal, spiritual gifts) Dzl.; also zaṅ-ziṅ (zang zing) by itself: what is earthly, pertaining to this world Mil. — 2. adj., confused in mind, stupefied Sch., v. the preceding article.

1. copper, γsér-zaṅs (gser zangs) gilt copper, zaṅs-kyi btsa (zangs kyi btsa) prob. verdigris. — 2. kettle B., C., v. ṕan-dil (phan dil); zaṅs-su skól-ba (zangs su skol ba) to boil in a kettle Dzl.; zaṅs kól-pa (zangs khol pa) a boiling kettle Dzl.; dkár-zaṅs (dkar zangs) bronze or brass kettle, lčags-zaṅs (lcags zangs) iron kettle. — zaṅs-rkyáṅ (zangs rkyang) copper can or jug. — zaṅs-skyógs (zangs skyogs) copper ladle. — zaṅs-čén (zangs chen) a large, zaṅs-čúṅ (zangs chung) a small kettle. — zaṅs-tíg (zangs tig) a small species of gentian. — zaṅs-tíb (zangs tib) copper tea-pot. — *zaṅ-ton-sa* (*zang ton sa*) W. copper-mine. — zaṅs-ṭál (zangs thal) copper slacks Glr. — zaṅs-mdóg (zangs mdog) copper colour. — zaṅs-sdér (zangs sder) copper plate or dish Sch. — zaṅs-snód (zangs snod) copper vessel. — *zaṅ-bu* (*zang bu*) C., W., = zaṅs (zangs) 2; *zaṅ-bu čé čuṅ nyi* (*zang bu che chung nyi*) two copper kettles, a large one and a little one. — zaṅs-ma (zangs ma) = zaṅs-bu? (zangs bu) Miṅ. — zaṅs-γya (zangs g.ya) Cs.: 'copper-green', prob. verdigris. — zaṅs-sa (zangs sa) copper-ore Cs.
south-western province of Ladak, zaṅs-dkar-pa (zangs dkar pa), -ma (-ma) man or woman of that province.

C. *zen* (*zen*), I. resp. bsáṅ-ma (bsang ma), also ḱam-zán (kham zan) Mil. 1. pap, porridge, of flour and water, thick, boiled or not boiled, warm or cold, also called bág-zan (bag zan), esp. as dough for baking; in C. porridge is gen. made of rtsám-pa (rtsam pa), and if possible of tea; _obrás-zan ('bras zan) rice-p., _oó-zan ('o zan) milk-p.; porridge being the daily food, as bread is with us, the word is used also 2. for food in gen: zan zá-ba (zan za ba) to take food, to eat, bdag daṅ zan mi zá-na (bdag dang zan mi za na) if you will not eat with me Dzl.; zan-dráṅ (zan drang) cold, zan-drón (zan dron) warm food, zan-čáṅ (zan chang) meat and drink, S.g.; zan btsos-pa (zan btsos pa) boiled food; *zan-kón* (*zan kon*) dearth W.; zan zos (zan zos) 1. he was eating porridge. 2. as one word: Bal. wife, cf. bza (bza); fig. lkog-zán zá-ba (lkog zan za ba) to take unlawful interest Sch. — 3. fodder, provender, v. bzan (bzan). — II. inst. of za (za) eater, as second part of a compound: śa-zán (sha zan) meat-eater; carnivorous animal Glr.; nya-zán (nya zan) fish-eater, ichthyophagist; ṕag-zán (phag zan) pork-eater.
silk, fine or heavy silk, v. dar-záb (dar zab); zab-čén (zab chen) costly silk cloth Sch.; zab-skúd (zab skud) Lt., Mil. silk-cord; zab-_oból (zab 'bol) silk covering for a seat, bolster Pth.

vb., adj. and sbst, to be deep, deep, depth, záb-po (zab po), gen. záb-mo (zab mo), adj., deep, frq.; often fig., blo-záb (blo zab) Cs.: a profound mind or understanding; zab-záb byas kyaṅ záb-mo raṅ mi _odug (zab zab byas kyang zab mo rang mi 'dug) although people call it deep, it is not deep Sch.; zab-lám (zab lam), záb-moi sgom-kríd (zab mo'i sgom khrid) a term of Buddhist mysticism, doctrine of witchcraft, = dbú-mai lam (dbu ma'i lam), or ṕyág-rgya čén-po (phyag rgya chen po). — zab-kyád (zab khyad) depth, = zabs (zabs), Dzl., Mil.
depth, zábs-su _odom bčui doṅ (zabs su 'dom bcu'i dong) a pit ten fathoms in depth.

bridge, grú-zam (gru zam) bridge of boats Cs.; lčágs-zam (lcags zam) iron bridge, wire-bridge; lčúg-zam (lcug zam) suspension-bridge, by means of cables of twisted birch-tree branches; _odrén-zam ('dren zam) draw-bridge Cs.; rdó-zam (rdo zam) 1. stone-bridge. 2. natural rock-bridge; rtswá-zam (rtswa zam) common expression for lčúg-zam (lcug zam) and tsár-zam (tsar zam); the latter: suspension-bridge by cables formed of thin split cane; śiṅ-zam (shing zam) wooden bridge; zám-pa _odzúgs-pa (zam pa 'dzugs pa) to throw a bridge Cs.; zám-pai ká-ba (zam pa'i ka ba) or rkáṅ-pa (rkang pa) the piers or foundations, spaṅ-léb (spang leb), spaṅ-sgó (spang sgo) the boards or planks, mda-yáb (mda' yab) or lag-rtén (lag rten) parapet, yźu-tóg (gzhu tog) arch, zam-ydúṅ (zam gdung) beam of a bridge, Cs.; zam-čén (zam chen) a large bridge, zam-čúṅ (zam chung) a little one Cs., zám-bu (zam bu) id.

1. supine of zá-ba (za ba); zar jùg-pa (zar 'jug pa) to give to eat. — 2. pitch-fork, for shaking up the corn, hay-fork, dung-fork; forks at dinner are not yet used in Tibet, spoons and knives, and in Lhasa chop-sticks, answering their end sufficiently.
Sch.: tassel; acc. to our authorities: gold-brocade.
Glr., Mil. seems to be tassel.
